questions.question: Define a self-referential structure and explain why it cannot contain an instance of itself directly.
keyPoints: Structure containing a pointer to another structure of same type
Cannot contain direct instance because it causes infinite recursion in size calculation
Pointers have fixed size breaking the recursive definition

questions.question: Write a C structure definition for a node of singly linked list storing integer data.
keyPoints: Use struct keyword with tag name
Include data member (int)
Include pointer member of same struct type
Set pointer name to next or similar

questions.question: Explain the purpose of NULL pointer in linked lists. What problems occur if NULL is not used properly?
keyPoints: NULL indicates end of list (last node's next)
Without NULL, traversal continues to invalid memory
Causes segmentation faults or infinite loops
Essential for detecting list termination

questions.question: Differentiate between singly linked list and doubly linked list structures.
keyPoints: Singly: one pointer (next) per node
Doubly: two pointers (next AND prev) per node
Doubly allows traversal in both directions
Doubly uses more memory but provides more flexibility

questions.question: What is memory leak in context of self-referential structures? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Memory leak: allocated memory becomes inaccessible
Occurs when head is reassigned without freeing old nodes
Prevented by calling free() before reassignment
Also occurs when nodes are not freed after removal

questions.question: Explain the arrow operator (->) and its importance when working with self-referential structures.
keyPoints: Arrow operator accesses members through pointer
Syntax: pointer_to_struct->member_name
Equivalent to (*ptr).member
Essential for navigating linked lists as nodes are accessed via pointers

questions.question: Write a C program to create a singly linked list of 3 nodes and display all node data. Explain each step.
keyPoints: Define structure with data and next pointer
Declare head pointer and initialize to NULL
Create first node: malloc, assign data, set next to NULL, head=node
Create second node: malloc, traverse to last, link last->next = new
Create third node: same process
Display: traverse from head, print data, move to next until NULL
Explain malloc returns NULL check, arrow operator usage, traversal logic

questions.question: Write a function to insert a new node at the beginning of a linked list. Explain the algorithm and its time complexity.
keyPoints: Function signature with head pointer reference
Allocate memory for new node using malloc
Check if malloc successful (not NULL)
Assign data to new node's data member
Set new node's next to current head
Update head to point to new node
Time complexity: O(1) - constant time
Space complexity: O(1) - only one new node

questions.question: Explain the difference between malloc() and calloc(). Why is it important to check the return value of malloc()?
keyPoints: malloc: allocates single block, memory uninitialized
calloc: allocates multiple blocks, all initialized to zero
malloc takes size in bytes, calloc takes number of elements and size each
Checking malloc return: prevents dereferencing NULL
Dereferencing NULL causes segmentation fault
Always check: if (ptr == NULL) { handle error }

questions.question: Explain what is a circular linked list. Write the structure definition and explain how traversal differs from regular linked list.
keyPoints: Circular: last node's next points back to head instead of NULL
Structure definition same as regular singly linked list
Traversal: continue until current->next != head (not until NULL)
Must handle case of single node pointing to itself
Used in round-robin scheduling, repeated operations
